worth_it_explanation: Samba TV reports arrive with reach figures, demographic breakdowns, and outcome numbers that can each be misread in a specific way: interim figures treated as final, sample-based projections read as census counts, or household-level reach confused with person-level reach. These checks confirm that the team's report-review cadence allows problems to be caught during the flight rather than after it, that the maturation window for in-flight figures is understood before conclusions are drawn, and that the unit of measurement for each headline metric matches how Samba TV actually defines it.
intro: Samba TV measures cross-screen reach, frequency, demographics, and outcomes using Automatic Content Recognition (ACR) data collected from opt-in smart TVs. Its reports may include both in-flight figures, which mature as more ACR data arrives from the panel, and a final report once the campaign has closed and the data has stabilized. Because the panel is a sample rather than a census of all viewers, headline metrics are projected estimates with associated confidence intervals, not exact counts. These checks confirm that the reporting cadence supports mid-flight course correction, that in-flight figures are read with their maturation status in mind, and that the reach and demographic figures in the report are interpreted against how Samba TV defines and derives them.

audit_checks.title: Samba TV reports are not reviewed during the flight, so delivery and audience problems surface only at campaign end
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Samba TV provides in-flight reporting specifically so delivery pacing, audience composition, and reach accumulation can be evaluated while the campaign is still running. Not reviewing that data until the flight ends removes the opportunity to reallocate budget, adjust targeting, or flag a delivery problem while there is time to act.

educate.base:
Samba TV delivers in-flight reporting during an active campaign alongside a final report once the flight ends and the dataset stabilizes. The in-flight view is intentionally preliminary: ACR data from the smart TV panel continues to arrive and is processed on a rolling basis, so figures for recent days may change as more data comes in. That maturation behavior does not make in-flight data unusable. It means the data should be read at a cadence that gives it time to stabilize for the window being reviewed, rather than pulling the dashboard hours after a flight begins and reading the first numbers as representative.

The value of in-flight data is that reach accumulation, audience composition, and pacing against the contracted delivery commitment can all be evaluated while adjustments are still possible. If reach is accumulating against the wrong demographic because the data provider's segment behaved differently than expected, or if delivery is pacing low because of inventory constraints, those are findings that can be acted on mid-flight. Waiting for the final report converts actionable findings into historical ones.

A structured review cadence does not need to be daily. For a four-week flight, a review at the end of week one and again at the end of week two gives the in-flight data enough time to settle for the earliest days while still leaving time to respond. The review should check at minimum: pacing toward contracted reach, demographic composition against the intended target, and whether any segment or placement is significantly under-delivering.

A few signs this check applies:
  • The team's first substantive look at Samba TV data happens after the campaign ends, when the final report arrives
  • No scheduled mid-flight review date was set when the campaign launched
  • The in-flight dashboard has not been accessed during the current flight, or the last access was more than two weeks ago for a campaign still running
You may want to set a specific review date for each active Samba TV campaign at launch, note it in the campaign brief, and confirm someone is responsible for pulling and reviewing the in-flight data at that date.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer running a Samba TV campaign tied to a seasonal promotion, an in-flight review midway through the flight gives you time to shift spend toward better-performing placements or confirm the demographic composition is trending toward the intended buyer before the sale window closes.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser using Samba TV to measure cross-screen reach ahead of a retargeting phase, an in-flight review lets you confirm that the target audience is accumulating at the expected rate before the retargeting campaign is scheduled to launch.

educate.learn_more_note: Samba TV in-flight reporting details are covered in campaign setup documentation available from the Samba TV account team. Contact your account representative for access.

investigate.intro: Confirm whether in-flight report reviews were scheduled and completed for the active or most recent campaign.

samba_tv.label: Samba TV
steps: Open the Samba TV reporting dashboard for the campaign. Confirm whether an in-flight report is available and when data was last reviewed.
Check the campaign brief or trafficking notes to see whether a mid-flight review date was documented at launch.
Review the dashboard access log or ask the team when the in-flight data was last pulled. Note how many days or weeks remain in the flight.
If no review has occurred and the campaign is more than one week old, note the gap and the remaining flight duration.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner confirms whether a mid-flight review was scheduled and completed for the campaign. A campaign with a documented review date and a confirmed data pull during the flight passes. A campaign where no in-flight review was scheduled or completed before the final report arrives is a fail, recorded with the campaign name, flight end date, and the date the Samba TV dashboard was last accessed.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether a mid-flight review date was set, whether it occurred, and the current access history for the Samba TV dashboard above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s Samba TV campaigns are not reviewed during the flight, so delivery pacing, audience composition, and reach accumulation are evaluated only after the campaign ends when no adjustments are possible. Samba TV provides in-flight reporting specifically to support mid-campaign decisions. {{voice_recommend}} setting a scheduled review date for each active Samba TV campaign at launch, noting it in the campaign brief, and confirming a named person is responsible for pulling and reviewing the in-flight data at that date.

audit_checks.title: In-flight Samba TV figures are treated as final before the data has matured
audit_checks.impact_rationale: ACR data from smart TV panels arrives and is processed on a rolling basis, so figures pulled shortly after a campaign period are preliminary. Reading an immature figure as settled leads to premature conclusions about reach or audience delivery that change when the data stabilizes, and those conclusions may trigger unnecessary campaign changes.

educate.base:
Samba TV's measurement relies on Automatic Content Recognition (ACR) data from a panel of opt-in smart TVs. ACR data does not arrive instantaneously. Smart TVs report viewing data on a delay, and Samba TV processes that data as it arrives, which means any figures pulled shortly after a campaign period are based on a partial dataset. This is the maturation period. For the most recent days in an in-flight report, the figures are the least complete and the most likely to change as additional data arrives.

The maturation behavior is most pronounced for very recent data, typically the last 24 to 72 hours of any reporting window, and it diminishes as more time passes after the measurement period closes. A report pulled 48 hours after a campaign day may undercount reach for that day. The same report pulled seven days later will show a more complete figure. Samba TV typically indicates in its reporting interface which figures are preliminary and which have reached a stable state, but this labeling is not always surfaced prominently in exported or shared versions of the report.

The practical consequence is that a campaign should not be evaluated against in-flight numbers pulled too close to the period they cover. A mid-flight review is most useful when it looks at data from periods that have had time to settle, typically data that is at least several days old, rather than data from the most recent 48 hours. Post-campaign evaluation should use the final report rather than the last in-flight snapshot. The final report is produced after the data has been given time to fully process.

A few signs this check applies:
  • A post-campaign analysis references in-flight report figures rather than the final Samba TV report
  • A campaign change, such as a budget reallocation or targeting adjustment, was made based on reach numbers pulled within 24 to 48 hours of the delivery period they cover
  • The team is unaware that Samba TV data matures after the measurement period and treats any pull from the dashboard as settled
You may want to confirm which figures in any shared report are flagged as preliminary, and whether the post-campaign analysis is based on the final report or a point-in-time in-flight snapshot.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer reviewing Samba TV reach figures ahead of a budget decision for the next flight, you may want to confirm the figures come from the final report or from an in-flight window that had adequate time to mature, rather than from a pull taken within a day or two of the most recent campaign period.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser using Samba TV reach data to size a retargeting audience, you may want to confirm the reach figure is from the final report, not from an in-flight snapshot, so the retargeting estimate is based on settled data.

learn_more.source_title: OTT/CTV and Server-Side Ad Insertion Digital Video Measurement Guidelines

investigate.intro: Confirm whether post-campaign analysis uses the final Samba TV report and whether any in-flight figures used for decisions came from a window that had time to mature.

samba_tv.label: Samba TV
steps: Open the Samba TV reporting dashboard. Check whether the report being used for post-campaign analysis is labeled as the final report or as an in-flight snapshot.

For any campaign decision made mid-flight, note the date the data was pulled and the date of the most recent campaign period the data covers. Calculate whether the gap was adequate for data maturation.
If the report includes a 'data as of' timestamp or preliminary indicator on specific metrics, note which metrics carry that label and whether the analysis treats them as settled.
Ask the team whether they are aware that ACR data matures after the measurement period and whether they apply a standard wait time before reading in-flight figures as representative.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner confirms whether post-campaign analysis uses the final report and whether any campaign decisions based on in-flight figures were made from data that had adequate time to stabilize. A campaign where the post-campaign analysis uses the final report and any in-flight decisions referenced data at least several days old passes. A campaign where post-campaign analysis uses a point-in-time in-flight snapshot, or where mid-flight decisions used data pulled within 24 to 48 hours of the measurement period, is a fail, recorded with the date of the data pull and the measurement period it covers.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether the post-campaign analysis uses the final report or an in-flight snapshot, and note the data pull date relative to the measurement period it covers, above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} is drawing conclusions from Samba TV in-flight figures that have not had adequate time to mature. ACR data from smart TV panels arrives on a delay and continues to process after the measurement period, so figures pulled close to the period they cover are incomplete and will change as additional data arrives. {{voice_recommend}} using the final Samba TV report for post-campaign analysis rather than a point-in-time in-flight snapshot, and when reviewing in-flight data, focusing on periods that are at least several days old rather than the most recent 24 to 48 hours.

audit_checks.title: Headline reach figures are misread because household reach, person-level reach, and projected estimates are not distinguished
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Samba TV reports may present household reach, person-level reach, and demographic reach as separate figures, each derived from sample-based projections with different denominators. Treating any of these as a census count, or conflating households with individuals, produces audience delivery claims that overstate or understate actual reach and mislead planning for subsequent flights.

educate.base:
Samba TV measurement is panel-based: data comes from a sample of smart TVs whose owners have opted in to ACR tracking. Reach and frequency figures are projections from that sample to the broader population, not direct counts of every person or household that saw an ad. This distinction matters when reading headline numbers.

Samba TV reports may present reach at more than one level of aggregation. Household reach counts the number of distinct households where at least one person was exposed. Person-level reach, where reported, estimates the number of distinct individuals. Because more than one person may watch the same TV, household reach is typically a smaller number than person-level reach. The two metrics are not interchangeable, and using the larger one as the delivery headline when a client was sold on household reach, or vice versa, produces a number that does not correspond to the contracted metric.

Demographic reach figures carry an additional layer of projection. Samba TV maps ACR viewing data to household demographics using probabilistic matching against third-party data sources. A figure described as 'adults 25-54 reached' is an estimate of how many individuals in that demographic segment were in a household where exposure was measured, projected to the broader population. The confidence interval on that figure widens as the demographic segment narrows. A reach claim for a very specific demographic cut should be read alongside the sample size it is based on, not as a precise count.

A few signs this check applies:
  • A post-campaign summary reports a single reach number without specifying whether it is household reach or person-level reach
  • A demographic reach figure, such as 'reached 4.2 million adults 18-34', is cited in a client report without noting that it is a projected estimate from a panel rather than a direct measurement
  • The team does not know which reach metric Samba TV uses as its default headline figure in the report or how it differs from the other reach figures in the same report
You may want to confirm which reach metric the Samba TV report uses as its headline figure, verify that it matches the metric sold to the client, and note in any shared report that demographic reach figures are projected estimates.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer whose Samba TV buy was scoped to household reach among a specific demographic, you may want to confirm that the post-campaign report uses household reach rather than person-level reach as the delivery headline, and that the demographic cut is labeled as a projected estimate.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser reporting cross-screen reach to a client, you may want to confirm whether the figure in the report is household reach or person-level reach, and include a brief note that the number is a projected estimate from Samba TV's opt-in panel rather than a census-based count.

learn_more.source_title: Cross-Media Audience Measurement Standards: Phase I Video

investigate.intro: Confirm which reach metric Samba TV uses as the headline figure in the report, whether household and person-level reach are distinguished, and whether demographic reach figures are labeled as projected estimates.

samba_tv.label: Samba TV
steps: Open the Samba TV final report for the campaign. Identify the headline reach figure and note whether the report labels it as household reach, person-level reach, or something else.

Check whether the report presents both household reach and person-level reach as separate rows or columns. If both are present, note the values and whether the campaign brief specified which metric was the contracted delivery unit.

Locate the demographic reach figures in the report. Check whether they are labeled as projected estimates or whether any confidence interval, margin of error, or sample size footnote accompanies them.
Compare the headline reach figure to the metric specified in the insertion order or campaign brief to confirm they match.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records which reach metric is used as the headline figure, whether it matches the contracted delivery unit, and whether demographic reach figures are labeled as projected estimates. A report where the headline reach matches the contracted metric and demographic figures are labeled as estimates passes. A report where the headline reach metric is unlabeled or does not match the contracted unit, or where demographic figures are cited as precise counts without an estimate caveat, is a fail, recorded with the metric name, the value shown, and the metric specified in the insertion order.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the headline reach metric name, its value, the metric specified in the insertion order, and whether demographic figures are labeled as projected estimates above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s Samba TV reports do not consistently distinguish between household reach and person-level reach, and that demographic reach figures are cited without noting they are projected estimates from a panel rather than direct counts. These are different metrics with different denominators; using them interchangeably produces audience delivery claims that cannot be verified against what the campaign was actually designed to deliver. {{voice_recommend}} confirming the headline reach metric in each Samba TV report matches the contracted delivery unit, adding a brief note in any client-facing report that demographic reach figures are sample-based projections, and reviewing the insertion order language to confirm the agreed delivery metric is the one Samba TV reports by default.
